text:
As everyone knows, autonomous agents are a staple of the academic and industry research communities. Developing agents typically involves a learning process where agents are trained in some training environment with some form of knowledge base. This process does not match the way humans learn, and therefore autonomous agents do not typically display human-level behavior. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown through example after example that an LLM that has been trained on a large amount of web knowledge can indeed exhibit very human-like intelligence. This blog post is about how we are starting to apply these new ideas to the area of autonomous agents.

This paper gives a systematic and comprehensive overview to this new field of research. First, we propose a unified theory of the design of Large Language Model (LLM) based intelligent agents that consists of four sub-modules: the profiling module, the memory module, the planning module, and the action module. Based on this theory, we discuss in-depth how the agents could learn specific abilities by using different methods of fine-tuning and mechanisms. Next, we give a full survey of applications of such agents in the social sciences, the natural sciences and engineering. Finally, we summarize the existing evaluation methods, which include both user-based evaluation and algorithm-based metrics. With this paper, we give an overview of the state-of-the-art of the research field on the design of the new type of agents and point out the open problems and future research works on such intelligent agents.
